  • Tutorial 1: Fitting LC Models to a CPW

Tutorial 1: Fitting LC Models to a CPW¶

This tutorial walks through the single-point workflow in simpleLOMs.system.

You will:

  • Define CPW geometry and coupling capacitances

  • Fit Foster, Optimized, and Analytical LC models

  • Run analyze_system() to compare all models against the CPW reference

  • Inspect resonance frequencies, linewidths, and circle-fit hybridization shifts

analyze_system() uses reflection circle fits on S11 and S22 for \(f_0\) and \(\kappa\), and for loaded-network mode shifts (primary metric: keys ending in CF).

[ ]:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import skrf as rf

from simpleLOMs import CPWParams, FosterFit, OptimizedFit, AnalyticalFit, analyze_system
from simpleLOMs.analysis import circle_fit_f0_kappa, resonance_from_s_max
from simpleLOMs.networks.cpw import cpw_resonator_network_2port
from simpleLOMs.plotting import plot_re_im, plot_lom_vs_data_re_im, plot_all_models
from simpleLOMs.models.optimized_fit import OptimizationConfig
from simpleLOMs.utils import make_results_table, make_params_table

print("Imports OK")

1. Device parameters¶

Define CPW geometry (CPWParams), coupling capacitances, and two LC loads representing qubits or auxiliary resonators.

In practice, extract Cc1, Cc2, Ctog1, Ctog2 from your EM solver (HFSS, COMSOL, etc.).

[ ]:

cpw_params = CPWParams(
    w=11.7e-6,
    s=5.1e-6,
    t=0.0,
    h=500e-6,
    rho=1e-19,
    ep_r=11.45,
    has_metal_backside=True,
    tand=0.0,
)

d = 7.0e-3
Z0 = 15.0
analytical_Z0 = 45.926

Cc1, Cc2 = 3.0e-14, 7.0e-14
Ctog1, Ctog2 = 4.0e-14, 6.0e-14

Lload1, Cload1 = 2e-9, 6.0e-13
Lload2, Cload2 = 4e-9, 6.0e-13

f_load1 = 1.0 / (2 * np.pi * np.sqrt(Lload1 * Cload1)) / 1e9
f_load2 = 1.0 / (2 * np.pi * np.sqrt(Lload2 * Cload2)) / 1e9
print(f"Load 1 bare resonance: {f_load1:.4f} GHz")
print(f"Load 2 bare resonance: {f_load2:.4f} GHz")

# Coarse band for initial fitting; analyze_system refines around the CPW resonance
freq = rf.Frequency(6e9, 9e9, 400_001, unit="Hz")

1a. Inspect the unloaded CPW network (optional)¶

[ ]:

cpw = cpw_resonator_network_2port(
    freq, d, Cc1, Cc2, Ctog1, Ctog2, cpw_params=cpw_params, Z0=Z0,
)
f0_s11, k_s11 = circle_fit_f0_kappa(cpw, m=0, n=0)
print(f"CPW circle-fit S11: f0={f0_s11/1e9:.4f} GHz, kappa={k_s11/1e6:.3f} MHz")

plot_re_im(cpw, m=0, n=0, quantity="re", title="CPW Re(S11)")

2. Fit each model individually¶

Three LC extraction methods are available. Each returns effective \(L\) and \(C\) you can reuse in circuit simulations.

2a. AnalyticalFit¶

Closed-form estimate from a specified resonance frequency and characteristic impedance.

[ ]:

from simpleLOMs.models.analytical_fit import AnalyticalFit

analytical = AnalyticalFit()
analytical.fit(freq, f_r=f0_s11, Z0=analytical_Z0)
analytical_net = analytical.get_network(freq, Cc1, Cc2, Ctog1, Ctog2, Z0=Z0)
print(analytical)
plot_lom_vs_data_re_im(analytical_net, cpw, m=0, n=0, lom_label="Analytical", data_label="CPW")

2b. FosterFit¶

Extracts \(L\) and \(C\) from the admittance slope at resonance (Phys. Rev. Lett. 108, 240502).

[ ]:

foster = FosterFit(cpw_params=cpw_params)
foster.fit(freq, d=d)
foster_net = foster.get_network(freq, Cc1, Cc2, Ctog1, Ctog2, Z0=Z0)
print(foster)
plot_lom_vs_data_re_im(foster_net, cpw, m=0, n=0, lom_label="Foster", data_label="CPW")

2c. OptimizedFit¶

Two-stage numerical optimization against CPW S-parameters. Usually the most accurate.

[ ]:

config = OptimizationConfig(verbose=False, n_widths=4)
optimized = OptimizedFit(config=config)
optimized.fit(
    freq,
    data_ntw=cpw,
    Cc1=Cc1, Cc2=Cc2,
    Ctog1=Ctog1, Ctog2=Ctog2,
    d=d,
    cpw_params=cpw_params,
    Z0=Z0,
)
opt_net = optimized.get_network(freq, Cc1, Cc2, Z0=Z0)
print(optimized)
plot_lom_vs_data_re_im(opt_net, cpw, m=0, n=0, lom_label="Optimized", data_label="CPW")

3. Full system analysis with analyze_system()¶

analyze_system() runs all three fits, extracts \(f_0\) and \(\kappa\) via reflection circle fits on S11/S22, builds loaded networks, and reports hybridization shifts.

The returned dict includes model objects (cpw_network, foster_model, …) and scalar metrics.

[ ]:

results = analyze_system(
    freq=freq,
    d=d,
    Cc1=Cc1, Cc2=Cc2,
    Ctog1=Ctog1, Ctog2=Ctog2,
    Lload1=Lload1, Cload1=Cload1,
    Lload2=Lload2, Cload2=Cload2,
    cpw_params=cpw_params,
    Z0=Z0,
    analytical_Z0=analytical_Z0,
    verbose=False,
)

scalar_keys = [k for k in results if not hasattr(results[k], "s")]
print(f"{len(scalar_keys)} scalar result keys")
print("Primary shift metric:", results["Optimized all shifts CF (GHz)"])
print("Shift error vs CPW:", results["Optimized shift errors CF (%)"])

3a. Summary tables¶

[ ]:

print(make_results_table(results))
print()
print(make_params_table(results))

3b. Overlay all models¶

[ ]:

plot_all_models(
    {
        "CPW": results["cpw_network"],
        "Foster": results["foster_network"],
        "Optimized": results["optimized_network"],
        "Analytical": results["analytical_network"],
    },
    m=0, n=0,
    quantity="re",
    title="Re(S11) from analyze_system()",
)

3c. Extract fitted parameters from model objects¶

Each fitted model exposes .get_params() for downstream circuit design.

[ ]:

for name in ["optimized_model", "foster_model", "analytical_model"]:
    model = results[name]
    print(name, model.get_params())

Next steps¶

  • Tutorial 2 — sweep coupling capacitances and load frequencies to map model accuracy (run_accuracy_sweep, run_accuracy_sweep_load_grid)

  • Tutorial 3 — explore geometry and circuit design with SweepConfig (sweeps.py)
